title: The Sower in the Boat
subtitle: Christ teaches the crowds from a little ship, scattering the seed of the Word

stillness: Be still, and let the seed of the Word fall into you.
As seed is hidden under the earth, so the Word of God must lie hidden in your memory, that it may bear fruit.
Quiet your soul, lest the seed be trampled in the passing of cares.
gospel: The Lord Jesus went out to the sea, to fish for men.
And because of the crowd gathered to him,
he went up with his disciples into a little boat,
and sitting in it, he taught the simple and devout crowds standing on the shore.
Behold, the sower went out to sow his seed.
And as he sowed, some fell beside the way, and the birds of the air devoured it.
Some fell upon rock, and when it sprang up it withered, because it had no moisture.
Some fell among thorns, and the thorns choked it.
And some fell upon good ground, and bore fruit a hundredfold.
behold: Behold the Lord Jesus going out to the sea, to fish for men.
Because of the crowd pressing upon him, he goes up into a little boat with his disciples.
Chrysostom says: he climbed into the boat, that he might leave no one behind him, but hold all his hearers before his face.
So that the people might hear him and see him, and the sight delight the hearing, and the hearing the sight.
And Bede says: this boat prefigured the Church, to be built up in the midst of the nations, in which the Lord consecrates for himself a beloved dwelling.
Behold him sitting in the boat, teaching the simple and devout who stand upon the shore.
See how he sets before the crowd four different parables, fitted to the differing conditions of men,
so that, as there are different sicknesses, there might be different medicines.
For some are delighted by bitter food, some by sweet, some by harsher, some by gentler.
He is like a rich father of a household, refreshing his guests with varied dishes,
that each one might receive his nourishment according to the nature of his own stomach.
consider: Consider that the sower is Christ himself; the preacher is rather the sower's basket than the sower.
He who is everywhere went out — not by changing place, but by the Incarnation;
and through the garment of flesh he was made nearer to us.
He scatters the Word without distinction, and it falls upon four kinds of ground.
Some falls beside the way: upon the heart that wanders through error, that strays through wantonness,
worn down by carnal affections and the suggestions of demons.
And the birds of the air — the demons — devour it; by their suggestion they snatch it away,
and take the Word and the memory of the Word from the heart, lest believing they be saved.
Some falls upon the rock: upon the stony, hard, rebellious heart, made stubborn through pride.
It springs up at once, but when the sun and heat of temptation rise, it withers,
because it had no depth of earth — that is, no steadiness of patience, no moisture of devotion and grace.
These hear, and are moved for a moment, but do not resolve to do what is said;
and like a tree often transplanted, they take no fixed root, but turn from good to evil.
Some falls among thorns: into hearts anxious with the craving for riches, torn by their worries,
given over too much to temporal cares through avarice.
For riches and honors are thorns: as thorns wound and bloody the body, and make it foul in the eyes of men,
so the love and ambition of riches lacerate the soul with the prickings of its own cares —
the hope of gaining, the fear of losing, the anxiety of keeping —
and when they drag it down even to sin, they leave it bloodied as by an inflicted wound, wretched and defiled.
Therefore the words of God must be held in the mind;
for, as Gregory says, just as we despair of the life of one whose failing stomach cannot keep its food,
so he must dread the peril of eternal death who does not hold the words of life in his memory.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, make me to go out from my old life,
that the seed of your Word be not trampled on the wayside of distraction,
nor wither on the rock of hard obstinacy,
nor be choked among the thorns of anxious care;
but rather, in the good and best ground of a humble, compassionate, and joyful heart,
may it bring forth the hundredfold fruit in patience.
Grant me also to understand and to do all these things spoken in parables,
and by word, or at least by example, to teach them to others. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Exiit autem qui ubique est, non localiter sed Incarnatione; et per amictum carnis propinquior nobis factus est.
latinJewel.english: He who is everywhere went out — not by changing place, but by the Incarnation; and through the garment of flesh he was made nearer to us.
